results.possibleIdentity: Giselle Mauer (sometimes referenced as Giselle Maher) is a Romanian-born sculptor who was active in the late 1960s and 1970s. She is historically associated with the non-profit organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which promoted collaborations between the arts and engineering. Mauer participated in E.A.T.'s significant 1968 exhibition, "Some More Beginnings: An Exhibition of Submitted Works Involving Technical Materials and Processes," held at the Brooklyn Museum. Born in 1930, she often worked with modern metal sculpture and collaborated with artist Ben-Ami Kadish (1923–2012). In the early 1970s, she was noted for unveiling a modern metal sculpture at the Hillel Center at Rutgers University and maintaining a studio in Elizabeth, New Jersey.

results.possibleIdentity: Judy Padow (also known as Judith Padow) is an American dancer, choreographer, and curator who was a significant figure in the New York postmodern dance and avant-garde art scenes from the late 1960s through the 1980s. She is best known for her long-standing collaboration with choreographer Lucinda Childs, performing in seminal minimalist works such as 'Calico Mingling' (1973). Padow was a founding member of the improvisational dance collective 'The Natural History of the American Dancer' alongside Carmen Beuchat, Barbara Dilley, Suzanne Harris, Cynthia Hedstrom, and Rachel Lew. Throughout the 1970s, she was deeply integrated into the downtown New York art community centered around the artist-run space 112 Greene Street. Her work often intersected with technological experimentation; she collaborated with artist Keith Sonnier on video and surveillance-based performances and was part of the creative circle associated with Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), specifically linked to the legacy of the '9 Evenings: Theatre and Engineering' project through her work with Childs. As a choreographer, she led the Judy Padow Dance Company, creating works such as 'Cameo,' 'Indian Summer,' and 'Remote Sensing,' which frequently featured music by minimalists like Philip Glass. Later in her career, she served as an administrator and curator, notably co-running the Danspace Project at St. Mark's Church in-the-Bowery with Cynthia Hedstrom.

results.possibleIdentity: Lillian Feldman Schwartz (1927–2024) was an American artist recognized as a pioneer of computer-mediated and digital art. Her career bridge between art and technology began in earnest in the mid-1960s when she joined the Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.) group, an organization dedicated to fostering collaboration between artists and engineers. One of her most significant early works was the kinetic sculpture 'Proxima Centauri' (1968), which was featured in the landmark Museum of Modern Art (MoMA) exhibition 'The Machine as Seen at the End of the Mechanical Age.' This sculpture, which used interactive sensors and light projections, later served as a prop in the original Star Trek television series. Following the success of 'Proxima Centauri,' Schwartz began a long-term residency at AT&T Bell Laboratories in 1969, where she worked as a 'resident visitor' and consultant for over 30 years. At Bell Labs, she collaborated with prominent figures such as computer graphics pioneer Ken Knowlton and computer music innovator Max Mathews. Together with Knowlton, she produced a series of early computer-animated films, including 'Pixillation' (1970), 'UFOs' (1971), and 'Enigma' (1972), which blended computer-generated imagery with traditional techniques like painting and collage. Beyond animation, Schwartz is well known for her digital analysis of classical art, particularly her 'Mona/Leo' (1987) experiment which suggested structural similarities between Leonardo da Vinci's self-portrait and the Mona Lisa. In 1984, she received an Emmy Award for a computer-generated television spot for MoMA. In 2021, her extensive archives, including films and personal papers, were acquired by The Henry Ford museum.

results.possibleIdentity: Robert Kragen (born c. 1945) is an artist and theorist active in the late 1960s and early 1970s, recognized as a pioneer in the use of television as a creative medium. He is closely associated with the organization Experiments in Art and Technology (E.A.T.), which facilitated his collaboration with electronics engineer Irv Rosner and artist Robert Lippman. Together, they produced the interactive electronic work "Come Near unto me" (also referred to as "Come Unto Me" or "Come Near Unto me Physically"), which was exhibited in the 1968 E.A.T. exhibition "Some More Beginnings" at the Brooklyn Museum. The piece used a color television to generate dynamic organic patterns of light and stereo sound in response to the movements of viewers. Kragen was a contributor to the first issue of the influential video art journal Radical Software in 1970, where he published the article "Art and TV," advocating for the use of sound and light as non-linear creative elements. He was also involved with the USCO (Us Company) intermedia group and the Morning Light Co. His background included an early interest in electronics and physics, having been noted for his science fair entries and guitar talent in his youth in South Orange, New Jersey.
